attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a pull request
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

490	def test_concurrent_init_of_distinct_conn_strs_does_not_serialize(fake_pg, monkeypatch):
491	    # #129: a stuck advisory-lock wait on conn_A must NOT block bootstrap of an unrelated conn_B.
492	    # We make conn_A's advisory lock block on an Event; conn_B (different conn_str) must still
493	    # complete. The pre-#129 code held a single process-global mutex across the advisory wait, so
494	    # conn_B would be blocked behind conn_A — this test discriminates that regression.
495	    import sys
496	    import threading
497	
498	    cp, state = fake_pg
499	    release_a = threading.Event()
500	    a_in_lock = threading.Event()
501	
502	    BlockPool = sys.modules["psycopg_pool"].ConnectionPool
503	
504	    class BlockingConn:
505	        def __init__(self, conn_str):
506	            self.conn_str = conn_str
507	
508	        def execute(self, sql, params=None):
509	            if "pg_advisory_lock" in sql and "5432/assay" in self.conn_str:
510	                a_in_lock.set()
511	                release_a.wait(5)  # hold the lock until released
512	
513	    class _PoolWrap(BlockPool):
514	        def __init__(self, conn_str, **kw):
515	            super().__init__(conn_str, **kw)
516	            self._conn = BlockingConn(conn_str)
517	
518	    monkeypatch.setattr(sys.modules["psycopg_pool"], "ConnectionPool", _PoolWrap)
519	
520	    # Resolve the conn_str by THREAD identity rather than racing on a shared os.environ key
521	    # (#F-026 / C-7): both threads previously mutated ASSAY_POSTGRES_URL, so B's write could land
522	    # before A read it, making both use one conn_str and the serialization assertion pass
523	    # vacuously. A per-thread resolver makes the two conn_strs deterministic and independent.
524	    conn_by_thread = {
525	        "init-a": "postgresql://localhost:5432/assay",
526	        "init-b": "postgresql://localhost:5433/other_db",
527	    }
528	    monkeypatch.setattr(
529	        cp,
530	        "get_postgres_connection_string",
531	        lambda: conn_by_thread[threading.current_thread().name],
532	    )
533	
534	    results: dict = {}
535	
536	    def init_a():
537	        cp.get_checkpointer()
538	        results["a"] = True
539	
540	    def init_b():
541	        a_in_lock.wait(5)  # ensure A is parked inside its advisory-lock wait
542	        cp.get_checkpointer()
543	        results["b"] = True
544	
545	    ta = threading.Thread(target=init_a, name="init-a")
546	    tb = threading.Thread(target=init_b, name="init-b")
547	    ta.start()
548	    tb.start()
549	    tb.join(5)
550	    assert results.get("b") is True, "conn_B blocked behind conn_A's advisory wait (#129)"
551	    release_a.set()
552	    ta.join(5)
553	    assert results.get("a") is True

181	def test_record_is_thread_safe_under_concurrency():
182	    # #114: concurrent record() must not corrupt the seq/prev_hash chain.
183	    # A naive concurrency test is non-discriminating under the GIL — the read->append window is
184	    # too narrow to interleave. We inject a clock that sleeps INSIDE that window (record() reads
185	    # seq/prev_hash, then calls the clock, then appends), so an UNLOCKED record() would let
186	    # threads read the same seq and corrupt the chain. With the lock (held across the whole
187	    # _record_locked, clock included) threads serialize and the chain stays intact. Verified
188	    # discriminating: removing the lock makes this fail (duplicate seqs / broken chain).
189	    import threading
190	    import time
191	
192	    def widening_clock():
193	        time.sleep(0.0005)  # widen the read->append window to force interleaving if unlocked
194	        return _dt.datetime.now(tz=_UTC)
195	
196	    t = ProvenanceTrail(clock=widening_clock)
197	    n_threads, per = 8, 15
198	    barrier = threading.Barrier(n_threads)
199	
200	    def worker(w):
201	        barrier.wait()  # release all threads at once → maximal contention in the window
202	        for i in range(per):
203	            t.record("k", f"w{w}-{i}", w=w, i=i)
204	
205	    threads = [threading.Thread(target=worker, args=(w,)) for w in range(n_threads)]
206	    for th in threads:
207	        th.start()
208	    for th in threads:
209	        th.join()
210	    assert len(t) == n_threads * per
211	    t.verify()  # intact hash chain despite concurrent appends through a widened window
212	    assert [e.seq for e in t.entries] == list(range(n_threads * per))  # contiguous, no dupes/gaps

142	def unfreeze(value: Any) -> Any:
143	    """Recursively convert a frozen structure back into plain JSON-serializable containers.
144	
145	    The inverse of :func:`freeze` for serialization: :class:`FrozenDict` → ``dict``,
146	    ``frozenset`` → sorted ``list`` (stable order for reproducible output), ``tuple`` →
147	    ``list``. Leaves are returned unchanged. ``json.dumps`` does not know :class:`FrozenDict`
148	    (it is a Mapping, not a ``dict`` subclass), so an operator serializing a deep-frozen public
149	    payload — e.g. ``GateReview.payload`` — must thaw it first (#141).
150	    """

152	        return {k: unfreeze(v) for k, v in value.items()}
153	    if isinstance(value, frozenset):

findings.title: G-004 in-process flaky guard: unfreeze(frozenset) order test mis-passes ~30% of runs against the buggy code
findings.description: test_unfreeze_frozenset_of_unorderable_types_is_deterministic (lines 43-48) asserts unfreeze(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) == [1,2,'a','b'] while running at the AMBIENT PYTHONHASHSEED (pytest does not pin it). The correct implementation sorts (src/assay_engine/_frozen.py:158-161), so the test passes today. But its stated job is to be a regression guard against a revert to raw set iteration (e.g. `list(value)`). As such a guard it is probabilistic: I enumerated 200 seeds and raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} equals the stable order [1,2,'a','b'] in 60/200 = 30% of seeds. So on the buggy raw-iteration code this test would still PASS ~30% of the time, depending only on the process hash seed. The test's own comment at lines 52-53 admits 'the in-process assertion above can pass ~1/3 of the time even on the buggy raw-iteration code.' Secondary defect: lines 47 and 48 use {1,'a',2,'b'} and {'b',2,'a',1}, which are the SAME set object — identical iteration order in any given process — so line 48 adds zero additional coverage and gives a false impression of testing two independent orderings.

findings.suggested_fix: Treat the in-process assertion as documentation only, not a guard; rely on the subprocess test (test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess) for discrimination. If keeping the in-process check, parametrize it to run under several pinned PYTHONHASHSEEDs that are known to differ from the stable order (re-run in subprocess), and replace the duplicate {'b',2,'a',1} set on line 48 with a genuinely different input (e.g. a 3-type mix like frozenset({1,'a',2.5,'b'})) so the two assertions actually cover distinct cases.

findings.title: Subprocess seed-pinned guard rests on a hardcoded, version-dependent iteration order that is already wrong on Python 3.14
findings.description: test_unfreeze_frozenset_order_is_seed_independent_subprocess pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 and the comment at lines 65-66 asserts 'raw frozenset iteration of {1,'a',2,'b'} is ['b',1,2,'a'] (verified)'. On the repo's interpreter (Python 3.14, .venv) the raw order at seed=2 is actually [1, 2, 'b', 'a'], not ['b',1,2,'a'] — the documented 'verified' fact is stale/wrong. The test's discriminating power depends on the chosen seed producing a raw order that DIFFERS from the stable [1,2,'a','b']; seed=2 happens to still differ on 3.14 (last two elements swapped), so the guard works by luck today, but the seed was empirically chosen against a specific CPython hash-randomization implementation. A future CPython that changes small-set hashing/iteration could make seed=2 coincide with the stable order (as 30% of seeds already do), silently turning this into a non-discriminating test that passes on the reverted buggy code. The guard is correct in intent but its single hardcoded seed is a hidden version dependency.

# seeds where raw == stable [1,2,'a','b'] (guard would be vacuous if one were chosen): 4,8,10,12,13,14,18,23,29,33,35,37,39,...
findings.suggested_fix: Do not depend on one hardcoded seed. Make the subprocess assert the property directly: in the child, compute raw=list(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) and stable=unfreeze(...); skip/retry seeds where raw==stable and require that for at least one seed where raw!=stable the unfreeze output still equals [1,2,'a','b']. Or loop over a handful of seeds (e.g. 0..9) and assert unfreeze() returns the stable order for every one, so no single seed's coincidence can mask a revert. Correct the stale 'verified' comment.

content.text:
I audited the ~520 tests in /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay for uncontrolled-nondeterminism flakiness. Findings recorded via StructuredOutput.

Summary of what I found and what I cleared:

Findings (2):
- PT-1 (high) — /Users/[REDACTED]/hc-assay/tests/test_immutability.py:47-48. The G-004-class in-process guard: `unfreeze(frozenset({1,'a',2,'b'})) == [1,2,'a','b']` runs at the ambient PYTHONHASHSEED. I empirically enumerated 200 seeds; raw set iteration coincides with the stable order in 60/200 = 30% of seeds, so if `unfreeze` reverts to raw iteration this test still passes ~30% of runs. Self-documented at lines 52-53. Bonus defect: lines 47 and 48 are the same set literal, so line 48 adds no coverage.
- PT-2 (medium) — test_immutability.py:65. The subprocess companion pins PYTHONHASHSEED=2 and claims raw order is `['b',1,2,'a']` "(verified)"; on the repo's Python 3.14 it is actually `[1,2,'b','a']`. The single hardcoded seed is a version-dependent assumption — 30% of seeds make raw == stable, which would render the guard vacuous on a future CPython hashing change.

Cleared as robust (not flaky):
- Timing assertions: test_reasoning_drain.py:140 (`< 5s` against a 0.3s inner timeout) and the `now() - 1 hour` clock-skew patterns are generously bounded.
- Concurrency tests (test_provenance.py:181, test_persistence.py:490/567, test_reasoning.py:259) use Events/Barriers and assert deterministic post-conditions (contiguous seqs, all-pools-closed); their sleeps only widen race windows and can only cause false negatives, not luck-based passes.
- Set/dict assertions in test_discovery.py and elsewhere compare against sets (order-independent), and FrozenDict hash equality (test_immutability.py:13) is order-independent by construction.
- Float comparisons in test_baseline.py use `pytest.approx`. The qdrant NN-ordering assert (test_persistence_live.py:65) uses an unambiguous query vector.
